skill.name: macos-spm-app-packaging
skill.description: Scaffold, build, package, sign, notarize, and release SwiftPM-based macOS apps without an Xcode project.
skill.summary: Create and release SwiftPM macOS app bundles using reusable shell templates.
skill.author: Dimillian

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many shell commands, filesystem writes, environment variable reads, keychain access, and placeholder URLs. Review confirms these are mostly expected for macOS packaging, signing, notarization, and Sparkle appcast generation, with no evidence of malicious intent or prompt injection. The skill should publish with a warning because release scripts handle credentials, private keys, temporary files, and macOS Keychain state.

medium_findings.title: Release Scripts Handle Signing Credentials
medium_findings.description: True positive for sensitive credential handling. The notarization script requires App Store Connect API credentials and writes the private key value to a temporary .p8 file before calling notarytool. This is legitimate for notarization, but it can expose secrets on shared systems if permissions, cleanup, or shell history are mishandled.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly reads notarization credential environment variables and writes key material to /tmp. The surrounding script purpose is legitimate Apple notarization, so this is a controlled medium risk rather than malicious exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Keychain and Certificate Modification
medium_findings.description: True positive for macOS Keychain access. The development signing helper creates a self-signed code-signing certificate, exports it as a temporary PKCS#12 file, and imports it into the login keychain. This changes local trust material and should only be run after user review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script explicitly invokes openssl and security import against the login keychain. The behavior matches the documented development signing purpose, but it modifies sensitive local certificate state.

medium_findings.title: Local Command Execution Templates
medium_findings.description: True positive for external command execution, but mostly expected. The templates run SwiftPM, codesign, lipo, xattr, find, open, pkill, xcrun, and Sparkle tooling to build and package a macOS app. The commands are local build operations, but users should inspect copied scripts before running them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple templates intentionally execute build, signing, process control, and release commands. The command set aligns with the skill description and does not show hidden network exfiltration or obfuscation.

medium_findings.title: Environment File Sourcing Requires Trust
medium_findings.description: True positive for environment file access. package_app.sh and sign-and-notarize.sh source version.env from the project root. This is a normal configuration pattern, but sourcing a shell file executes its contents if the project is untrusted.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts use shell source on a project-local env file. The template version.env is simple, but the pattern can execute arbitrary shell if a user copies scripts into an untrusted repository.

low_findings.title: Static Cryptography Flags Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: False positive for weak cryptographic algorithm claims in Swift package metadata and code-signing commands. The flagged lines reference Swift tools version, lipo, codesign, OpenSSL certificate generation, or Sparkle ed25519 keys rather than weak hashing such as MD5 or SHA1 for security decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no weak hash use at the cited locations. Some flags appear triggered by version strings or signing terminology, while setup_dev_signing.sh uses rsa:4096 with sha256.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Network URLs
low_findings.description: False positive for hardcoded network destinations. The URLs are example Apple plist DOCTYPE and placeholder Sparkle feed or download URLs. The scripts do not make HTTP requests to those URLs by themselves, although generated appcast metadata may reference user-provided hosting.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited URLs are defaults or XML identifiers, not evidence of data transfer. Network-capable release tooling is still present through notarization and user-hosted appcast workflows.

low_findings.title: Filesystem and Temporary File Writes Are Expected Build Artifacts
low_findings.description: Mostly false positive for filesystem risk. The scripts create .app bundle folders, .build entitlements, icon assets, temporary appcast directories, and release zip files as part of packaging. The notable caveat is that temporary credential files should remain short-lived and protected.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are transparent build outputs under the project or temporary directories. No evidence found of hidden persistence, path harvesting, or unauthorized destructive behavior.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal and Reconnaissance Flags Are Benign Context
low_findings.description: False positive for path traversal and reconnaissance. package_app.sh adds a standard @executable_path relative rpath for bundled frameworks, and the scripts use uname, pgrep, and spctl for build architecture, launch checks, and Gatekeeper validation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious strings are standard macOS packaging and validation mechanics. They do not traverse user-supplied paths to escape a sandbox or collect system inventory.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Run Shell Scripts With Local System Effects
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill ships shell scripts that users copy and execute. These scripts can build code, kill matching app processes, write app bundles, modify signing artifacts, and call Apple release tools.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: This is a clear and intentional capability of the skill. It is not hidden, but it has real local system effects that justify marketplace warnings.

dangerous_patterns.title: Secrets Passed Through Environment Variables
dangerous_patterns.description: Release workflows require secret values in environment variables for notarization and Sparkle signing. Users should keep these values out of repositories and avoid running the scripts in untrusted shells.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The scripts explicitly consume sensitive credential variables and key file paths. The usage is documented and appropriate for release automation, but still sensitive.

content.value_statement: SwiftPM macOS apps need manual bundle, signing, and release steps outside Xcode. This skill provides templates and references for building app bundles, notarizing releases, and preparing Sparkle appcasts.
actual_capabilities: Scaffolds a minimal SwiftPM macOS app with Package.swift and a SwiftUI entry point.
Builds release binaries for one or more macOS architectures using SwiftPM.
Assembles a .app bundle with Info.plist, resources, frameworks, icons, and entitlements.
Supports ad hoc, Developer ID, and development code-signing workflows.
Provides scripts for notarization, stapling, zip creation, and Sparkle appcast generation.
Documents version, architecture, signing, and release environment variables.
limitations: Requires macOS with SwiftPM and Apple command line tools for full packaging workflows.
Does not replace Xcode project features such as asset catalogs or complex build settings.
Release scripts require valid Apple Developer and App Store Connect credentials.
Templates must be reviewed and customized before use in production releases.

use_cases.title: Bootstrap a New Utility App
use_cases.description: Start a small SwiftUI or AppKit app from a SwiftPM layout without creating an Xcode project.

use_cases.title: Automate Release Packaging
use_cases.description: Create repeatable scripts for app bundle assembly, architecture checks, signing, notarization, and zip output.

use_cases.title: Prepare Sparkle Updates
use_cases.description: Generate appcast metadata and release notes artifacts for apps that use Sparkle outside the Mac App Store.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Starter App
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to scaffold a SwiftPM macOS app named MyApp. Include the files I need to build and run it locally.

prompt_templates.title: Customize Bundle Settings
prompt_templates.prompt: Adapt the packaging templates for my app name, bundle identifier, minimum macOS version, and menu bar mode.

prompt_templates.title: Set Up Release Signing
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my SwiftPM app structure and configure the packaging, signing, notarization, and zip release steps.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Sparkle Release Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a release workflow that builds a universal app, notarizes it, creates a Sparkle appcast, and prepares GitHub release assets.

output_examples.input: Package my SwiftPM app as a macOS .app bundle.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step packaging plan with script placement, app metadata values, build commands, signing mode, and expected bundle outputs.

output_examples.input: Add notarization to my release process.
output_examples.output: A release checklist covering Developer ID identity, App Store Connect credentials, notary submission, stapling, validation, and final zip creation.

output_examples.input: Generate a Sparkle appcast for a new version.
output_examples.output: Instructions for setting the Sparkle key path, release version, download URL prefix, release notes file, and appcast upload location.

best_practices: Review each copied shell script before running it, especially signing and release scripts.
Store Apple credentials and Sparkle keys outside the repository and inject them only during release.
Increment BUILD_NUMBER for every Sparkle update and validate the notarized app before publishing.
anti_patterns: Do not run release scripts from an untrusted repository or with unreviewed version.env content.
Do not commit App Store Connect keys, Developer ID credentials, or Sparkle private keys.
Do not ship the example bundle identifier, app name, feed URL, or placeholder signing identity.

faq.question: Does this skill require Xcode?
faq.answer: Full release workflows need Apple command line tools. Icon generation may require an Xcode installation.

faq.question: Can it package a SwiftUI app?
faq.answer: Yes. The bootstrap template includes a minimal SwiftUI app and the packaging scripts handle SwiftPM executables.

faq.question: Can it build universal binaries?
faq.answer: Yes. The templates support arm64 and x86_64 builds and combine outputs when multiple architectures are selected.

faq.question: Does it notarize apps automatically?
faq.answer: It provides a notarization script, but you must supply valid Apple credentials and review the release settings.

faq.question: Is Sparkle required?
faq.answer: No. Sparkle appcast generation is optional and can be removed if your app uses another update channel.

faq.question: What should I check before publishing?
faq.answer: Check bundle identifiers, entitlements, signing identity, version numbers, notarization status, and appcast URLs.
